Senses.DefinitionEn: Happen, take place
Senses.ExampleEn: There is a debate going on right now between the two parties.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Proceed to do or tackle STH after doing STH else
Senses.ExampleEn: Does anyone have any questions before I go on to the next chapter?

Senses.DefinitionEn: Get or take SB/STH from a place
Senses.ExampleEn: Can you pick up some food on the way home from work please?

Senses.DefinitionEn: Return to a place or a conversation topic
Senses.ExampleEn: She came back to the kitchen with a bottle of fancy wine.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Bring forth or produce
Senses.ExampleEn: She instantly came up with a solution to the problem.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Be happening soon (esp. be broadcast soon)
Senses.ExampleEn: Coming up after the news, our cooking program will feature cheese.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Return to a place, time, situation, activity, conversation topic
Senses.ExampleEn: He washed the dishes and went back to his room.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Discover STH; obtain knowledge of STH
Senses.ExampleEn: We need to find out who did this to her.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Leave a place (room, building, container) or appear from it
Senses.ExampleEn: She went into the bank and came out with some money.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Become known or revealed after being kept secret
Senses.ExampleEn: The news came out that he was leaving the team.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Make public knowledge a privately held position
Senses.ExampleEn: People need to come out and say what they think about it.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Become available or released to the public (film, record, book)
Senses.ExampleEn: Their new album is coming out next month.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Leave a room, building, car, or one’s home to go to a social event
Senses.ExampleEn: We should go out for dinner sometime.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Used as an intensifier, to highlight the active nature of what is being done
Senses.ExampleEn: Do you think he’ll go out and buy the whole company?

Senses.DefinitionEn: Direct attention toward STH (fact, idea, information)
Senses.ExampleEn: Experts have pointed out that eating too much sugar is extremely unhealthy.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Gradually advance in age and maturity
Senses.ExampleEn: Seeing my kids growing up is such a lovely thing.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Establish or create STH; arrange for STH to happen or exist
Senses.ExampleEn: An advisory committee is being set up.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Place STH in a particular spot or position
Senses.ExampleEn: We need to set up a few more chairs so everyone can sit down.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Prove or be discovered to happen or be
Senses.ExampleEn: Her suspicion turned out to be justified.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Leave a container (vehicle, room, building) or make SB/STH leave a container
Senses.ExampleEn: These prisoners have no hope of ever getting out of jail.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Enter a place or area (room, building)
Senses.ExampleEn: She opened the door and he came in.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Become involved in a situation
Senses.ExampleEn: We need experts to come in and give us advice.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Undertake or handle (role, task, responsibility, problem, issue)
Senses.ExampleEn: Nobody was willing to take on such an awful job.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Acquire or assume as one’s own (quality, meaning, colour, shape)
Senses.ExampleEn: The story takes on a whole new meaning when you read it again.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Stop doing or having STH; abandon (activity, belief, possession)
Senses.ExampleEn: She had to give up smoking when she got pregnant.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Form the whole of an amount or entity
Senses.ExampleEn: Hispanics make up more than 15% of the U.S. population.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Compensate for STH lacking, lost or missed
Senses.ExampleEn: Some solution has to be found to make up for such losses.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Make a decision
Senses.ExampleEn: You should make up your mind about who you will vote for.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Finally do STH or be in a particular place, state, or situation after doing STH or as a consequence of it, esp. unexpectedly
Senses.ExampleEn: She ended up having to sell her car after her accident.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Return to a place, position, state, activity, conversation topic
Senses.ExampleEn: She got back to London last Monday.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Raise one’s eyes
Senses.ExampleEn: He looked up from his book and shook his head.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Come to understand or determine STH
Senses.ExampleEn: Despite her efforts, she couldn’t figure out what had happened.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Move from a standing position to a sitting position
Senses.ExampleEn: Please sit down and have a drink.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Rise or cause to rise after lying in bed or sitting/kneeling
Senses.ExampleEn: She got up out of her chair and put on her shoes.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Remove STH/SB from somewhere (container or abstract whole)
Senses.ExampleEn: He tore open the envelope and took out a few bills.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Invite to a recreational place or social event
Senses.ExampleEn: You should take her out to this new Chinese restaurant.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Obtain an official document or service from an authority
Senses.ExampleEn: I had to take out a loan to cover all my expenses.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Said to encourage SB to try harder, or do or say STH
Senses.ExampleEn: Come on, don’t be shy and tell us your story.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Said to show SB disbelief, disagreement, or anger
Senses.ExampleEn: Oh come on, you're just lying to me!

Senses.DefinitionEn: Move down to a lower level or position
Senses.ExampleEn: After hitting the iceberg, the ship began to go down.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Decrease in value or amount
Senses.ExampleEn: I don’t think prices will go down.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Go from one place to another, esp. one that is further south or underneath
Senses.ExampleEn: We went down to Australia last year.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Make an appearance at a social or professional gathering
Senses.ExampleEn: She didn’t show up at the meeting.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Remove STH (esp. piece of clothing or jewellery from one’s body)
Senses.ExampleEn: I took off my shirt and went to bed.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Leave a place, especially suddenly
Senses.ExampleEn: They jumped into the car and took off.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Leave the ground and rise into the air
Senses.ExampleEn: The plane took off at 7am.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Plan, devise or think about STH carefully or in detail
Senses.ExampleEn: We still need to work out the details of the procedure.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Exercise in order to improve health or strength
Senses.ExampleEn: He works out at the gym five times a week.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Happen or develop in a particular way
Senses.ExampleEn: Everything worked out well in the end.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Prove to be successful
Senses.ExampleEn: Despite our efforts, it just didn’t work out.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Rise to a standing position after sitting or lying down
Senses.ExampleEn: He pushed away from the table and stood up.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Make public knowledge a privately held position
Senses.ExampleEn: Somebody’s got to stand up and say what’s wrong with this country.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Move from a higher spatial location to a lower one; fall/land onto the ground
Senses.ExampleEn: Come down from the roof or you will hurt yourself.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Reduce itself to one particular thing that is the most important or essential matter
Senses.ExampleEn: What it all comes down to is that the rules have not been respected.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Become lower in amount or value
Senses.ExampleEn: Interest rates are currently coming down.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Proceed with a course of action without further hesitation
Senses.ExampleEn: Go ahead and ask me your question!

Senses.DefinitionEn: Become higher in value; increase
Senses.ExampleEn: Oil prices have gone up last year.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Move upward, or from a lower spatial location to a higher one
Senses.ExampleEn: He could see a few hands go up in the audience.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Think of STH again; reconsider STH past
Senses.ExampleEn: Looking back on those days, we had a very happy life.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Look at STH/SB again after having momentarily looked elsewhere
Senses.ExampleEn: He closed the dictionary and looked back to his notes.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Become (or make SB become) conscious again after being asleep
Senses.ExampleEn: I was so tired that I woke up at 10 this morning.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Perform or complete (task, activity, study, experiment, attack, duties, etc)
Senses.ExampleEn: The experiment was carried out by a well-known academic.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Put into execution; implement (plan, idea, wishes, orders, views, etc)
Senses.ExampleEn: Economic reform will soon be carried out.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Gain control, management, or possession of STH/SB (task, job, political party, organisation)
Senses.ExampleEn: After her father died, she took over the company.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Hold STH in a high position (e.g. above one’s waist or head), so it can be seen or reached
Senses.ExampleEn: The professor held up the picture so everyone could see it.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Remain strong or in a fairly good condition after a bad period or the wear of time (person, business, device)
Senses.ExampleEn: These are really old shoes but they’re holding up quite well.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Delay or prevent the progression of STH/SB
Senses.ExampleEn: We were held up by heavy traffic.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Take STH/SB out of a container, thing or place
Senses.ExampleEn: He reached in his pocket and pulled out a gun.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Move so as to face in the opposite direction
Senses.ExampleEn: She turned around and walked out the door.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Make STH become better or more successful than it previously was (economy, business)
Senses.ExampleEn: People have stopped believing the President could turn around the economy.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Use a particular amount of space, time or effort
Senses.ExampleEn: The rewriting of the document took up a whole afternoon.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Discuss or deal with (issue, idea, matter)
Senses.ExampleEn: The Senate will take up the issue tomorrow.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Start doing a particular job or activity, esp. for pleasure
Senses.ExampleEn: He took up gardening last year.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Grasp an object, often moving it from a lower to a higher position
Senses.ExampleEn: I have to take up the carpet before I start hoovering.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Lower one’s eyes to see what is below
Senses.ExampleEn: She looked down at the ground to see what she stepped on.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Display or attach STH (e.g. to a wall) so it can be seen
Senses.ExampleEn: They put up a few posters on the wall.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Be willing to accept STH unpleasant or not desirable; tolerate
Senses.ExampleEn: I won’t put up with your bad behaviour for much longer.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Build or place STH somewhere
Senses.ExampleEn: They’re putting up a new fence after the previous one fell apart.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Make STH/SB return to a place, state, situation, or conversation topic
Senses.ExampleEn: This will bring back war into the country.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Bring STH one has taken from a place they come from
Senses.ExampleEn: This is the hat he brought back from South America.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Raise for discussion or consideration
Senses.ExampleEn: I didn’t think he would bring up the subject.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Care for/be responsible for a child until it becomes an adult
Senses.ExampleEn: She brought up her children under very difficult circumstances.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Look outside, or at the horizon
Senses.ExampleEn: She liked to go by the window and look out at the garden.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Take care of SB and make sure they are well; protect SB’s interests
Senses.ExampleEn: We look out for each other as if we were family.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Bring STH to a place or situation
Senses.ExampleEn: I brought in my laptop computer today because my office computer is broken.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Ask SB to do a particular job or task
Senses.ExampleEn: He had been brought in to save the company.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Make STH become available or possible, less limited
Senses.ExampleEn: This opened up opportunities he would never have imagined.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Open STH (door, gate, book, bag)
Senses.ExampleEn: She opened up the bag and grabbed some documents.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Have a look at; examine STH/SB (esp. to get more information or make a judgement)
Senses.ExampleEn: Check out our website for more information.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Start doing or discussing STH new (job, activity, conversation topic)
Senses.ExampleEn: Let’s move on to our next topic.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Change physical location (spot, room, country)
Senses.ExampleEn: She lived in New York, then London, and finally moved on to Rome.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Forget about a difficult experience and move forward mentally/emotionally
Senses.ExampleEn: He’s had a difficult year but he’s now ready to move on.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Make STH known or accessible to the public (information, products)
Senses.ExampleEn: Police have put out a warning about thieves in the area.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Stop STH from burning or shining
Senses.ExampleEn: The fire has finally been put out.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Place STH somewhere in order for it to be seen or used
Senses.ExampleEn: I’ve put out some glasses and a bottle of wine.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Examine a place or one’s surroundings so as to view what it might contain or look for a particular thing
Senses.ExampleEn: They entered the shop and looked around but nobody was there.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Become involved in STH which prevents SB from making progress or moving forward
Senses.ExampleEn: He is very busy and always caught up in his work.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Reach SB that is ahead by walking, running, or driving faster
Senses.ExampleEn: She was running so fast that it was impossible to catch up with her.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Reach the same level or standard as SB who is more advanced
Senses.ExampleEn: They made considerable improvements, which makes it hard for us to catch up.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Enter (place, area, room, building)
Senses.ExampleEn: This restaurant looks really nice; let’s go in and have lunch.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Stop working or functioning; fail or collapse (vehicle, device, relationship, negotiations)
Senses.ExampleEn: Our car broke down yesterday.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Divide or separate into categories or smaller components so as to make it easier to understand or deal with
Senses.ExampleEn: Let’s break down the task into three easy steps.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Lose control of one’s emotions and yield to tears or distress
Senses.ExampleEn: He broke down at his son’s funeral.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Undergo chemical decomposition; separate into different substances
Senses.ExampleEn: Digestion breaks down food into small molecules.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Go away from, leave (train, bus, aircraft, lift)
Senses.ExampleEn: You need to take the bus and get off at the third stop.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Begin something in a certain way
Senses.ExampleEn: The team has got off to a good start this season.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Manage to avoid serious trouble or consequences (esp. legal punishment)
Senses.ExampleEn: It’s not right that he could commit such a crime and get off so easily.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Move, progress or increase at the same rate or pace as SB/STH
Senses.ExampleEn: Workers’ income has not kept up with inflation.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Make STH continue
Senses.ExampleEn: This is amazing; keep up the good work!

Senses.DefinitionEn: Place STH/SB on the floor or on a flat surface
Senses.ExampleEn: She put down her glass and left the bar.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Stretch an arm in order to hold, touch, or get STH that is within short distance
Senses.ExampleEn: She reached out for the empty jar on the table.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Make an effort to address or communicate with SB, so as to help them or involve them in STH
Senses.ExampleEn: The government’s efforts to reach out to right-wing voters have paid off.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Go somewhere, esp. for a particular purpose
Senses.ExampleEn: He decided to go off to college.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Emit a loud noise or sudden light as a signal or warning
Senses.ExampleEn: Let’s hope the alarm doesn’t go off.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Explode (bomb) or be fired (gun)
Senses.ExampleEn: They could hear bombs going off at a distance.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Remove a part of STH by cutting it
Senses.ExampleEn: Take the carrots and cut the ends off.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Interrupt SB as they are speaking
Senses.ExampleEn: The teacher cut off the student in the middle of her sentence.

Senses.DefinitionEn: End the provision of STH, or be deprived of a provision (supply, money)
Senses.ExampleEn: The government decided to cut off food supplies.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Turn around so as to face the opposite direction
Senses.ExampleEn: Before leaving through the door, he turned back to kiss her goodbye.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Go back (or make SB/STH go back) in the direction SB/STH has come from
Senses.ExampleEn: When the storm hit, we had to turn back.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Stop or cause a vehicle to stop
Senses.ExampleEn: A van pulled up in front of them.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Move STH/SB from a lower position to a higher one; lift from the ground
Senses.ExampleEn: She pulled up her scarf to cover her cold face.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Start doing or working on STH, esp. with a particular goal in mind
Senses.ExampleEn: I set out to discover the truth behind the story.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Start a journey
Senses.ExampleEn: We set out for San Francisco on the following day.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Explain or present STH clearly, esp. officially and in writing
Senses.ExampleEn: The official recommendations were set out in the document.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Get rid of dirt, mess, pollution, or chemical substances in a place or area
Senses.ExampleEn: Make sure you clean up your mess because I won’t do it for you.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Make STH free from dangerous, unacceptable or controversial activities or contents
Senses.ExampleEn: He was asked to clean up his bad language during his interview.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Stop (or make STH stop) working or operating (machine, computer, business, premise, strategy)
Senses.ExampleEn: You should shut down your computer at night to save electricity.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Surrender possession or control to SB/STH (esp. in authority)
Senses.ExampleEn: The policeman turned over the criminal to the jail guard.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Change position so that the other side is facing towards the outside or the top, or another direction
Senses.ExampleEn: Put the chicken on the grill and turn it over a few times.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Move, proceed or progress at a slower pace (vehicle, economy)
Senses.ExampleEn: Economic growth has dramatically slowed down.

Senses.DefinitionEn: End up in a particular situation, condition or place, esp. an unpleasant one
Senses.ExampleEn: They wound up having to pay off his debts.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Yield; be (or make STH be) found, discovered, or noticed
Senses.ExampleEn: The search turned up solid evidence against him.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Increase the volume or level of STH
Senses.ExampleEn: I really like this song; could you turn up the radio?

Senses.DefinitionEn: Arrive or make an appearance somewhere
Senses.ExampleEn: He turned up to the meeting half an hour late.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Form or make SB/STH form into a line (also figurative)
Senses.ExampleEn: Dozens of taxis were lined up at the entrance.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Take STH/SB to a place, or time period (fig.), they were in before
Senses.ExampleEn: After dinner, he took her back to her house.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Regain possession or control over STH
Senses.ExampleEn: The politician’s ultimate goal is to take back the Senate.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Describe or explain STH clearly or in detail, esp. officially and in writing
Senses.ExampleEn: The whole strategy was laid out in detail in a twenty-page document.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Spread STH out on a flat surface, so it can be seen or used
Senses.ExampleEn: He laid out the plates on the table.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Move towards a place or person, esp. by crossing an area (room, city, country)
Senses.ExampleEn: She went over to the window so she could watch the scene.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Examine or discuss each part of STH in detail in order to understand or remember it better, or make sure it is correct
Senses.ExampleEn: We need to go over the list once again.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Finish a conversation on the telephone by putting the receiver down or switching the phone off
Senses.ExampleEn: He hung up the phone without letting her answer his question.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Experience STH difficult or unpleasant
Senses.ExampleEn: You have to understand the tough situation she went through before judging her.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Be officially accepted or approved
Senses.ExampleEn: I hope the tax cut goes through next year.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Refuse to let go of STH
Senses.ExampleEn: He held on to his job until the very last day.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Wait for a short time
Senses.ExampleEn: I’ll be quick, please hold on for one minute.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Pay the complete amount of STH
Senses.ExampleEn: It will take a dozen years for him to pay off his debts.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Pay back the effort spent in doing STH by becoming profitable or effective
Senses.ExampleEn: All the hard work will pay off in the end.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Move one’s hand or an object in one’s hand forward or towards SB, in order to grab or give STH
Senses.ExampleEn: He took the keys and held them out to her.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Hold STH as likely to happen or succeed (hope, possibility, prospect, promise)
Senses.ExampleEn: We don’t hold out much hope of finding the murderer.

Senses.DefinitionEn: End or cause to end or fail (esp. relationship)
Senses.ExampleEn: Their marriage broke up in

Senses.DefinitionEn: 2. Divide into smaller parts or components
Senses.ExampleEn: The USSR broke up into more than 10 countries.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Make a particular detail, quality or feeling more noticeable than it usually is
Senses.ExampleEn: This haircut brings out the natural curl in your hair.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Make SB or STH available for the public or an audience to see, know or buy
Senses.ExampleEn: The band was about to bring out their new album.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Take STH/SB out of a container or enclosed space
Senses.ExampleEn: They brought out another plate from the kitchen.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Move backwards or make SB/STH move backwards
Senses.ExampleEn: She pulled back the hair from her face.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Withdraw or retreat from an activity or location, esp. military
Senses.ExampleEn: The army was forced to pull back due to bad weather.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Wait for a short time
Senses.ExampleEn: Please hang on for a minute, I’ll be quick.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Refuse to let go of STH
Senses.ExampleEn: He hung on to his job until the very last day.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Increase or cause STH to increase, accumulate, or strengthen, especially progressively
Senses.ExampleEn: Tension was building up among competitors.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Refuse to accept or consider (esp. by people of authority)
Senses.ExampleEn: The president attempted to have the death penalty thrown out.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Put STH in a rubbish bin
Senses.ExampleEn: He threw out a dozen empty boxes that were piled up in the room.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Make SB leave a place, activity or organization, esp. forcibly and unexpectedly
Senses.ExampleEn: Several students were caught cheating and subsequently thrown out of school.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Spend time relaxing or enjoying oneself
Senses.ExampleEn: I don't like to hang out with people I work with.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Put a piece of clothing or jewellery onto one’s body
Senses.ExampleEn: You should put on your gloves, it’s really cold outside.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Present or stage (play, show, competition)
Senses.ExampleEn: They put on such an incredible show last night!

Senses.DefinitionEn: Begin to pay serious attention to STH
Senses.ExampleEn: We should get down to discussing those issues as soon as possible.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Lower one’s body as by kneeling, sitting or lying
Senses.ExampleEn: Get down on your knees so you can get a better view.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Come down from STH; descend (car, horse, tree)
Senses.ExampleEn: He loves climbing trees but finds it hard to get down.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Come to a place or area (spot, room, town, country), esp. towards SB or to join SB
Senses.ExampleEn: Could you come over and give me a hand with this?

Senses.DefinitionEn: Settle into a new house or place
Senses.ExampleEn: He liked the house so much that he decided to move in immediately.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Go towards SB/STH, esp. to attack or take control of them
Senses.ExampleEn: The assault was led by Lieutenant Jones, moving in from behind the hill.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Start a life, existence, profession, or course of action in a particular way or by doing a particular thing
Senses.ExampleEn: She started out as a shop assistant and gradually climbed the employment ladder.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Speak or utter loudly
Senses.ExampleEn: He could hear a voice call out his name.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Rise from a lying to a sitting position
Senses.ExampleEn: The sudden noise made her sit up in her bed and listen.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Refuse or dismiss (request, offer, opportunity)
Senses.ExampleEn: This is an opportunity you would be foolish to turn down.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Move or drive backwards a short way
Senses.ExampleEn: He got into his car and backed up out of the alley.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Take action in order to support STH or make it happen
Senses.ExampleEn: Politicians often fail to back up their words with actions.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Establish as valid or genuine
Senses.ExampleEn: You have to back up your accusations with solid evidence.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Move STH/SB to a place, position, or state they were in before
Senses.ExampleEn: Could you put the milk back in the fridge please?

Senses.DefinitionEn: Mail, send or distribute to a number of people
Senses.ExampleEn: Hundreds of copies were sent out to the local population.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Send SB to a place for a particular purpose
Senses.ExampleEn: Military troops were sent out to secure the region.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Go (or make STH/SB go) inside a place (car, house, room)
Senses.ExampleEn: The new security lock prevents thieves from getting in.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Get involved in an exciting or profitable activity/opportunity
Senses.ExampleEn: You should get in on the act!

Senses.DefinitionEn: Explode or destroy STH with a bomb, or cause to be exploded or destroyed
Senses.ExampleEn: Several attempts were made at blowing up official buildings.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Continue to do or be involved with STH, or make STH continue (especially despite difficulty)
Senses.ExampleEn: I would like to carry on working after I retire.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Engage or take part in
Senses.ExampleEn: His illness makes it difficult for him to carry on conversations.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Start on a trip or journey
Senses.ExampleEn: We will finish packing and set off in the morning.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Cause a device to explode, or a signal to start, esp. by accident
Senses.ExampleEn: He accidentally set off my car alarm.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Make STH happen or emerge, esp. without intending to
Senses.ExampleEn: Employees started to protest, setting off a dispute over workers’ rights.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Continue doing STH without stopping, or repeatedly
Senses.ExampleEn: She wiped tears off her cheeks but kept on crying.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Use STH (or become used) completely so that nothing is left
Senses.ExampleEn: We’ve run out of biscuits.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Leave suddenly, as if in a hurry
Senses.ExampleEn: After the argument, she ran out into the garden and screamed.

Senses.DefinitionEn: See or hear with difficulty
Senses.ExampleEn: I could barely make out his face in the dark.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Represent as being a particular way, esp. falsely
Senses.ExampleEn: He was innocent, but the media made him out to be a criminal.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Deal with a difficult situation successfully
Senses.ExampleEn: We were lucky to make it out of the war alive.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Stop (or make SB/STH stop) talking or making a noise
Senses.ExampleEn: Just sit down and shut up!

Senses.DefinitionEn: Stop a piece of equipment working temporarily or a supply flowing by turning a tap, pressing a button, or moving a switch
Senses.ExampleEn: People were asked to turn off their phones.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Cause to feel intense dislike
Senses.ExampleEn: His speech turned off left-wing voters.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Cause to happen or emerge, esp. STH positive
Senses.ExampleEn: This decision will bring about change in the political sphere.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Move back by lifting one’s foot and putting it down backwards
Senses.ExampleEn: He stepped back when the big man threatened him.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Stop being involved in STH so as to consider it more carefully/objectively
Senses.ExampleEn: We need to step back and take a broader perspective on the past events.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Put STH away or down on a surface, esp. because one has stopped using it
Senses.ExampleEn: I laid down my book and stood up.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Lie flat on a surface, usually to rest
Senses.ExampleEn: He laid the child down on the bed and wished her good night.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Lay the foundations of; establish or create
Senses.ExampleEn: The principles of good conduct were laid down decades ago.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Cause SB/STH to move downward or fall to the ground
Senses.ExampleEn: The rocket attack brought down the airliner.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Reduce the level, rate, or amount of STH
Senses.ExampleEn: The company’s expenses need to be brought down.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Cause SB/STH in a position of power (government, president, system, organisation) to lose its power/status
Senses.ExampleEn: They helped bring down one of the most corrupt dictatorships in history.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Distinguish oneself/itself by being better, more significant or more impressive than other people/things
Senses.ExampleEn: Excellent product quality is what made the brand stand out from its competitors.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Be easily seen or noticeable
Senses.ExampleEn: Flashing lights make planes stand out at night.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Appear or arrive; come into existence
Senses.ExampleEn: Such an opportunity comes along only once in a lifetime.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Go somewhere with SB
Senses.ExampleEn: We’re going to the cinema tonight; you should come along with us!

Senses.DefinitionEn: Happen or develop; be enacted or performed
Senses.ExampleEn: The way these negotiations play out will have important consequences.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Start suddenly, esp. STH undesirable and unpleasant
Senses.ExampleEn: Riots broke out that night.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Go from one place/person to another; circulate
Senses.ExampleEn: There is a rumour going around that she is pregnant.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Leave a place or event, especially suddenly or angrily
Senses.ExampleEn: She walked out of the meeting feeling irritated by her colleagues.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Succeed in reaching a physical destination or stage
Senses.ExampleEn: The food supplies never got through to the local population.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Be successfully communicated or understood
Senses.ExampleEn: He needed to speak slowly and clearly so his message would get through to the audience.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Succeed in contacting SB on the telephone
Senses.ExampleEn: I cannot seem to get through to the customer service department.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Overcome STH, esp. difficult or unpleasant
Senses.ExampleEn: He gave me useful advice, which helped me get through this difficult situation.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Decide not to do or say STH
Senses.ExampleEn: They should not hold back from joining us if they want to.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Prevent SB/STH from reaching their full potential
Senses.ExampleEn: You cannot let a few unmotivated pupils hold back the rest of the group.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Prevent SB/STH from going somewhere
Senses.ExampleEn: Security guards tried to hold back the crowd.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Contain an unwanted physical manifestation (tears, laughter, sigh, sneeze)
Senses.ExampleEn: She was holding back the laughter with great effort.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Record information on paper
Senses.ExampleEn: You should write down his contact details in case you want to get in touch.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Return to a place one has lived in before
Senses.ExampleEn: We moved back to New York last year.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Complete a form or official document
Senses.ExampleEn: We had to fill out a dozen forms in total.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Rest in a comfortable position against the back of a seat
Senses.ExampleEn: She sat back in her chair and turned on the TV.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Deliberately take no action/remain passive about STH
Senses.ExampleEn: We won’t just sit back and watch the situation getting worse and worse.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Exclude STH as a possibility, plausible cause or explanation
Senses.ExampleEn: They ruled out the possibility of a mass murder.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Move to a better position; advance to a higher level/rank
Senses.ExampleEn: She moved up from secretary to senior manager in just a few years.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Move upward, from a lower spatial location to a higher one
Senses.ExampleEn: She put her hand on his shoulder and moved it up along the back of his neck.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Choose SB/STH among a number of alternatives
Senses.ExampleEn: She picked out the best-looking dress she could find.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Detect/be noticed among a group of things or people
Senses.ExampleEn: My mum could easily be picked out in the picture.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Remove STH that was previously put up or put in place
Senses.ExampleEn: After the exhibition, they took the paintings down.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Destroy, kill, or disable
Senses.ExampleEn: The terrorists tried to take down the President's plane.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Take SB to a place, esp. further south or at a lower level
Senses.ExampleEn: My dad decided to take us down to Florida.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Continue doing STH after stopping
Senses.ExampleEn: We might as well get on with it if we want to finish on time.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Get on board some form of public transportation (train, bus, plane, elevator)
Senses.ExampleEn: He got on the bus to go to school.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Return STH to its original owner/provider
Senses.ExampleEn: It’s nice to be able to give back to the community.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Give STH to SB by holding it in one’s hand and offering it to them
Senses.ExampleEn: She turned around to hand over her keys to her husband.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Surrender control or responsibility for STH/SB to SB else, esp. officially
Senses.ExampleEn: The government isn’t willing to hand over power to local authorities.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Express or represent the most important/representative facts, ideas, or characteristics of SB/STH, especially in a brief manner
Senses.ExampleEn: He summed up the whole discussion in just a few minutes.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Leave one’s place of residence permanently
Senses.ExampleEn: Our neighbour is going to move out next month.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Become detached or removed from a larger whole
Senses.ExampleEn: The button is coming off my shirt.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Appear or seem to be a particular way
Senses.ExampleEn: He was tired and not prepared, and so came off poorly in the interview.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Be finished with STH; have completed STH
Senses.ExampleEn: The team just came off an incredibly successful season.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Circulate or communicate; give STH to SB after receiving it from SB else (information, ideas, object)
Senses.ExampleEn: I got this message this morning and was asked to pass it on to you.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Transmit from one generation to the next (traditions, beliefs, skills, possessions)
Senses.ExampleEn: These ancient traditions have been passed on from generation to generation.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Die (euphemism)
Senses.ExampleEn: She has been very depressed since her mother passed on.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Provide a place for SB to live or stay
Senses.ExampleEn: The family took her in when she was abandoned by her parents.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Fully understand or grasp the meaning of STH
Senses.ExampleEn: You have to explain more thoroughly; it’s too difficult to take in.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Deceive by behaving in a dishonest way
Senses.ExampleEn: He was very convincing, so I was easily taken in.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Put something on a surface or on the ground
Senses.ExampleEn: He carried the bags to his room and set them down.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Do what is needed to solve a problem, conflict or difficult situation
Senses.ExampleEn: A few ideas were raised to sort out the company’s financial issues.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Find out information so as to understand STH
Senses.ExampleEn: He will need some time to sort out the reasons for his failure.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Take action about STH after a previous action or thing, esp. so as to reinforce its effect
Senses.ExampleEn: You won’t be cured immediately after the operation; you will have to follow up with therapy.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Try to find more information about STH
Senses.ExampleEn: Detectives are following up on a few promising leads.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Be clearly perceived, noticed or seen (feeling, emotion, quality)
Senses.ExampleEn: Her disappointment came through by the tone of her voice.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Reach success or a desired goal despite difficulty
Senses.ExampleEn: He worked really hard, and despite some difficulties, he came through in the end.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Arrive at a destination; come into view (train, ship)
Senses.ExampleEn: We had to wait for a ship to come through and rescue us.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Adopt a quieter and steadier lifestyle
Senses.ExampleEn: I just want to fall in love with the right guy and settle down.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Become calmer, quieter, more orderly
Senses.ExampleEn: We need things to settle down before we can make a serious decision.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Get into a comfortable position, either sitting or lying
Senses.ExampleEn: When he reached the top of the hill, he settled down in the grass to have a rest.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Come in the area near STH/SB
Senses.ExampleEn: He came around to my room and kissed me goodnight.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Convert to an opinion or decision
Senses.ExampleEn: I believe she will come around to our way of thinking eventually.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Happen again as a regular event, at its usual time
Senses.ExampleEn: You’ll have to wait until summer comes around.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Do SB’s work temporarily because they cannot or will not do it themselves
Senses.ExampleEn: I had to fill in for her yesterday because she was ill.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Give SB extra or missing information they want or need
Senses.ExampleEn: She filled Carol in on the plan.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Put material or substance into STH in order to make it full or complete
Senses.ExampleEn: All the remaining holes had to be filled in with concrete.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Give to each of a large number of people
Senses.ExampleEn: The committee gave out more than 100 copies in the last meeting.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Make known openly or publicly
Senses.ExampleEn: You should be more careful and not give out your phone number so easily.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Collapse/fail; stop functioning properly (heart, knees)
Senses.ExampleEn: At 95 years of age, her heart finally gave out.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Cease resistance to (liking/temptation/habit, or to SB’s demands/control)
Senses.ExampleEn: She shouldn’t give in to her children’s demands.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Progress or proceed with an activity
Senses.ExampleEn: You will learn as you go along.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Act in cooperation or express agreement
Senses.ExampleEn: The Democrats are not likely to go along with the plan.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Go to a place or event, esp. without much planning
Senses.ExampleEn: Would you like to go along with us to the party?

Senses.DefinitionEn: Separate a part (or become separate) from a larger piece
Senses.ExampleEn: He accidentally broke off a piece of wood from the fence.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Stop speaking, especially suddenly
Senses.ExampleEn: “There is something that…” He broke off abruptly.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Put an end to STH (relationship, discussion, talks, negotiations)
Senses.ExampleEn: They broke off diplomatic relations in

Senses.DefinitionEn: Delay until a later time or date
Senses.ExampleEn: Now that I had more free time, there was no excuse to put off exercising any longer.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Cause to feel intense dislike
Senses.ExampleEn: The bad smell put everyone off.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Take place or happen/occur, esp. unexpectedly
Senses.ExampleEn: I did not expect this to come about.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Stop operating or functioning
Senses.ExampleEn: Non-profitable companies were closed down.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Put one thing inside another; include or insert
Senses.ExampleEn: You need to put in your contact details in case there is a problem.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Invest or devote so as to achieve STH (time, effort, work)
Senses.ExampleEn: I put in ten hours a day at the office.

Senses.DefinitionEn: Begin a course of action, usually with a specific purpose/objective in mind
Senses.ExampleEn: We set about laying the table before our guests arrived.
